ideas.Water as the principle (archê) of all th:
The claim that "**all things are water**" — that water is the *archē*, the fundamental principle or origin of everything — is the doctrine for which Thales of Miletus is famous, and it is often taken to mark the very **beginning of Western philosophy and science**. Thales held that water is the underlying source and nature of all things: the primary substance from which everything comes, of which everything is ultimately composed, and into which everything returns.

Reported by Aristotle (our main source), the doctrine may seem naive, but its significance is profound and lies less in the specific answer than in the *kind of question* Thales asked and the kind of answer he gave. He sought a **single, unifying material principle** underlying the bewildering diversity of the natural world — the idea that beneath all the varied things we see, there is one basic stuff. This search for unity beneath multiplicity, and for a permanent reality behind changing appearances, set the agenda for all subsequent Greek natural philosophy.

Why *water*? Ancient sources suggest plausible reasons: water is essential to all **life** and to the nourishment of living things; it takes multiple forms (solid ice, liquid, vapor), showing how one substance can transform; the seeds and sustenance of all things are moist; and the earth was thought to float on water. Water thus offered an observable, everyday substance capable, one might argue, of producing the variety of nature. Whatever the reasoning, Thales's importance is monumental: by proposing a **natural** first principle discoverable by reason and observation, he inaugurated the Milesian tradition and the whole project of explaining the cosmos through nature rather than myth.
ideas.Search for a natural rather than mythica:
Thales's deepest and most revolutionary contribution — the achievement that earns him the title "**father of philosophy**" (and of science) — was not any particular doctrine but a fundamental *shift in the kind of explanation* he sought: the turn from **mythological** to **naturalistic** accounts of the world. This move, initiated by Thales and the Milesians, is one of the great watersheds in intellectual history.

Before Thales, the peoples of the ancient world explained natural phenomena through **myth and the gods**. Earthquakes were the anger of Poseidon; lightning was hurled by Zeus; the origin of the world was told in stories of divine births and battles. Explanation meant recounting the deeds and wills of anthropomorphic deities. Thales broke with this entire framework. He sought to explain the natural world in terms of **natural causes and principles** — inherent features of nature itself, discoverable by human **reason and observation** — rather than by appeal to the arbitrary will of gods. A famous illustration: rather than attributing earthquakes to a god, Thales reportedly proposed that the earth floats on water and that earthquakes result from its being rocked by waves — a naturalistic (if incorrect) hypothesis of exactly the new type.

The importance of this lies in its founding assumptions: that the cosmos is an ordered, intelligible system governed by discoverable regularities rather than divine caprice, and that human beings can understand it through rational inquiry. This is the birth of the very idea of **nature** (*physis*) as an autonomous domain, and of philosophy and science as the rational study of it. Every subsequent naturalistic inquiry — the whole tradition of Western science — flows from this decisive Milesian turn away from myth toward reasoned explanation.
ideas.Early geometry and astronomy:
Beyond his cosmological speculations, Thales of Miletus was renowned in antiquity as a **mathematician** and **astronomer** — one of the legendary "Seven Sages" of Greece — and the achievements attributed to him mark early steps in the transformation of practical, empirical knowledge into **theoretical, demonstrative science**. Whether or not every specific accomplishment is historically accurate (the sources are late and partly legendary), the tradition surrounding Thales reflects his reputation as a pioneer of rational inquiry into number, shape, and the heavens.

In **astronomy**, Thales was famously credited with **predicting a solar eclipse** — traditionally the eclipse of 585 BCE, which reportedly halted a battle between the Lydians and the Medes. Whatever the truth of the story, it symbolizes the new confidence that celestial events follow regular, predictable patterns discoverable by reason rather than being unpredictable divine portents. He is also said to have studied the solstices, estimated the sizes and distances of the sun and moon, and given practical advice on navigation by the stars.

In **geometry**, Thales is credited (notably by later Greek tradition) with introducing deductive geometry to Greece, possibly drawing on Egyptian and Babylonian practical mathematics but raising it toward general, provable propositions. Several theorems bear his name, including "**Thales's theorem**" (that an angle inscribed in a semicircle is a right angle) and results about the bisection of a circle and the base angles of an isosceles triangle. He is famously said to have measured the **height of the pyramids** by their shadows and the distance of ships at sea. These achievements, real or embellished, made Thales an emblem of the emergence of mathematics and astronomy as rational sciences, and of the Milesian faith in an ordered, knowable cosmos.

works.No writings survive; testimonies in Aris:
Like several of the earliest Greek thinkers, **Thales of Miletus left no writings** that survive — and it is uncertain whether he wrote anything at all. Everything we know about the man traditionally honored as the **first philosopher** comes to us **indirectly**, through the reports (*testimonia*) of later authors who mention, summarize, or discuss his views. This absence of any first-hand text makes Thales a shadowy, semi-legendary figure, and reconstructing his actual thought a matter of careful and cautious interpretation.

Our single most important and authoritative source is **Aristotle**, who lived some two and a half centuries after Thales. In his *Metaphysics* and *De Anima*, Aristotle discusses Thales as the founder of the tradition of natural philosophy that sought a material first principle (*archē*), reporting his doctrine that water is the origin of all things and that "all things are full of gods," and cautiously reconstructing Thales's possible reasons. Because Aristotle is early, philosophically acute, and relatively close to the tradition, his brief reports are invaluable — though he is interpreting Thales through his own conceptual framework.

A second major source is **Diogenes Laërtius**, whose *Lives of the Eminent Philosophers* (third century CE) is a compendium of biography, anecdote, doctrine, and gossip about the ancient thinkers. From him and other doxographers (like Simplicius and the later "opinion-collectors") come the many colorful stories associated with Thales: his prediction of an eclipse, his cornering of the olive-press market to prove philosophers *could* be rich, his falling into a well while gazing at the stars, and his supposed geometrical and astronomical feats. These later reports are often unreliable, mixing genuine tradition with legend. Sifting fact from embellishment across this indirect, second-hand transmission is the central challenge in understanding the founder of Western philosophy.
